author: bridge-bot
content:
<b1afra> re: **@chrishobcroft**
> When are we gonna get a trustless light client running on a watch? Is it a pipe-dream, or is it even incoming? I don't need an exact date / fork / etc. obviously, I just want a feeling from you.
Why on a watch? What kind of watch (iOS or Android)? Why not on a phone? When do you only have your watch with you? 
Can you be more specific, what you would need it to be able to do? Just sync committee bootstrap and updates? And then verifying proofs?
And how would you envision a user interface?

author: bridge-bot
content:
<stkux> re: **@chrishobcroft**
> When are we gonna get a trustless light client running on a watch? Is it a pipe-dream, or is it even incoming? I don't need an exact date / fork / etc. obviously, I just want a feeling from you.
With colibri, this is possible right now. It needs about 250k only. It runs on microcontrollers (Esp32 is enough). So, a smartwatch should have enough resources.

With colibri, you don't need any sync. You just get the data and the proof. That means, only, if you need verifiable information, you have traffic. 

Do you have a use-case where it could be integrated?

author: bridge-bot
content:
<chrishobcroft> Use case: the watch is your only battery-powered personal device. It's your daily-driver

It can be attached your wrist/your person, with little screen for doing little things like reading messages, navigating, confirming. It's connected to headphones with a natural language Voice User Interface (VUI) e.g. "Hey Claude help me and Dirk.eth meet up now for tea at a comfy tea shop" for on-the-go usage.

Then for more visually-immersive HCI, the screen can be mirrored (wired/wirelessly) to a (touch)screen, with optional keyboard/mouse, for Phone/Tablet/Desktop/Movie-Watching User Interface e.g. a phone-like peripheral, a tablet-like peripheral, a laptop-like peripheral, a keyboard-mouse-monitor-like peripheral set, a TV/projector-with-remotecontrol-like peripheral set, a car-like peripheral.

Why on a watch? Harder to steal off your wrist compared to a phone. Smaller potential cost entry-point for mass adoption.

Which kind of watch (iOS or Android)? Could it conceivably be neither? Maybe Linux/BSD? Something risc-y? And I am not at all talking about these (actually-dumb) "smart" watches which are essentially gimmicky peripherals to the "actually-smart" phone, I mean an "actually-smart" watch with own network stack and sovereignty.

Why not a phone? I mean, you would still be able to mirror it to a phone-like peripheral if you are so addicted to this kind of pacifier.

You only have your watch with you whenever you want to have it on, whenever you want to have a way to sign, to communicate, to navigate, to talk to.

The main usage I consider right now for having this synched to the chain is for verifying the anchors of your human-to-human connections.

Understood. My use case for the watch is different. The only time I don't have my phone with me, but my watch. Is when I swim or jog. I use the watch almost exclusively as a companion to my pjone. Or the other way round. 
Technical <clipped message>

author: bridge-bot
content: <chrishobcroft> They don't really. But with Google closed-sourced WearOS and Apple being Apple, I think we will have an even harder time infecting those devices with chain stuff than we have with phones. So we need a different approach.

author: bridge-bot
content:
<stkux> re: **@chrishobcroft**
> They don't really. But with Google closed-sourced WearOS and Apple being Apple, I think we will have an even harder time infecting those devices with chain stuff than we have with phones. So we need a different approach.
I don't expect that this will be more complicated than having a stateless client in a smartphone app. As such an app can communicate with an RPC, it also can fetch data with proofs - so, technically it is not a problem.
